Glossary About
Early Modern

Elisabeth of Bohemia

1618 – 1680

I will treat this as a report on Elisabeth’s own philosophy of mind, not merely as a footnote to Descartes.

I will treat this as a report on Elisabeth’s own philosophy of mind, not merely as a footnote to Descartes. Her texts do not give us a treatise, so the “system” has to be reconstructed from letters, questions, objections, and practical concerns. That reconstruction should be cautious: some theses are explicit, some are strongly implied, and some are disciplined extrapolations from recurring commitments.

The central thesis I would extract is this: Elisabeth develops a naturalistic, embodied, non-reductive philosophy of the human soul, one that refuses both crude material reduction and a purely intellectualist definition of mind as mere thought. The human soul is not adequately understood if one abstracts it from bodily condition, affective life, action, illness, habit, fortune, and providential dependence. The mind may retain autonomy in thought and will, but its actual human exercise is bodily and situationally conditioned. This makes her philosophy of mind at once metaphysical, physiological, moral, therapeutic, and theological.

1. Sources, scope, and method

Elisabeth Simmern van Pallandt, Princess Palatine of Bohemia, lived from 1618 to 1680. Her extant philosophical writings consist almost entirely of her correspondence with Descartes, begun at her initiative in 1643 and continuing until Descartes’s death in 1650. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy stresses both the narrowness of the textual base and the breadth of her concerns: mind-body causation and union, physics, passions, virtue, freedom of the will, divine causal determination, and political philosophy. Descartes dedicated the Principles of Philosophy to her and wrote The Passions of the Soul at her request.

This means that any “Elisabethian philosophy of mind” must be reconstructed from a correspondence. But that does not make it merely fragmentary or derivative. The surviving letters show positive philosophical commitments concerning causation, the nature of mind, natural explanation, virtue, and governance. They also show an unusual ability to press metaphysical theses against physiology, medicine, ethics, and lived experience.

The word “soul” should not be narrowed too quickly into the contemporary analytic category “mind.” In the seventeenth-century context, âme includes intellect, will, consciousness, passions, sensation, immortality, moral agency, and theological status. Elisabeth’s question is therefore not just “How can mental events cause physical events?” It is closer to: What must the human soul be, if it thinks, wills, feels, suffers, reasons, moves the body, is moved by the body, is subject to illness, and yet may also be immortal and answerable to God?

2. The first and decisive problem: voluntary action

Elisabeth’s first famous question is not a generic query about “interaction.” It is specifically about voluntary action. She asks how the soul, being “only a thinking substance,” can determine the bodily spirits so as to produce voluntary bodily actions. She reasons from the mechanistic physics Descartes himself accepts: determination of corporeal motion appears to require impulse, contact, or surface configuration; contact seems incompatible with an immaterial thing, and extension is excluded from Descartes’s notion of the soul.

A recent German reconstruction presents the argument as a reductio: assume the Cartesian soul is thinking, immaterial, and non-extended; assume the soul determines bodily motion in voluntary action; assume corporeal motion requires either impact/contact or shape/extension; then the soul both must and cannot determine bodily motion. The conclusion is not necessarily that no soul exists, but that Descartes’s concept of the soul is inadequate for what voluntary action requires.

This is philosophically important. Elisabeth is not merely saying “two different substances cannot interact.” Her objection is more exact. She is asking how a pure thinking substance can produce the determinate corporeal changes required for voluntary action under Cartesian mechanics. If the will is to move the body, it must do more than “occasion” movement vaguely; it must determine the course of animal spirits or bodily motions in a structured way. That makes her problem one of agency, causal interface, and psychophysical control.

Descartes’s first response is to distinguish three primitive notions: body is understood through extension, shape, and motion; soul through thought; and soul-body together through union, which grounds the soul’s power to move the body and the body’s power to cause sensations and passions in the soul. He also warns against explaining one primitive notion by another, especially against explaining soul-body union by body-body mechanics.

Elisabeth’s dissatisfaction is revealing. She does not accept “primitive union” as sufficient. A primitive notion may describe how we experience ourselves, but it does not yet explain how voluntary bodily action is possible. Her standard is stricter: a philosophy of mind must be compatible with the best natural philosophy of causation available. That is why she is often read as committed to efficient, mechanistic causal explanation and suspicious of scholastic appeals to occult or formal qualities.

3. The request for a better definition of the soul

Immediately after the causal question, Elisabeth asks for “a more precise definition of the soul” than Descartes gives in the Meditations: she wants the soul’s substance distinguished from its action, namely thought. She notes that the inseparability of soul and thought is hard to prove in cases such as fetal life and deep fainting.

This point is often underplayed, but it is central. Elisabeth is not only challenging Descartes’s account of how soul and body interact. She is challenging the identification of soul with thought. If the soul is a substance, and thought is its action, then defining the soul by thought risks confusing the bearer with one of its operations. A theory of mind must say what the soul is when it is not clearly thinking, when thinking is suspended, disordered, latent, embryonic, or physiologically impaired.

Eric Stencil’s recent analysis usefully separates three problems in Elisabeth’s early letters: the causal interface problem, the vapors problem, and the principal-attribute problem. On this reading, Elisabeth’s deepest target is not interaction alone but Descartes’s picture of the soul as a purely thinking thing whose only proper modes are thinking modes.

The “principal attribute” issue matters because it pushes beyond familiar dualism debates. Elisabeth is not simply choosing between “mind is immaterial” and “mind is matter.” She is asking whether thinking is the essence of the soul in such a way that the soul cannot be understood apart from actual or potential acts of thought. Her fetal and fainting examples imply that a human soul may have modes, capacities, conditions, or properties not exhausted by conscious cognition.

4. The vapors problem: embodied cognition and pathological dependence

Elisabeth’s second major challenge concerns cases in which bodily disorder affects the capacity to reason. She writes that it is difficult to understand how a soul, after having the faculty and habit of reasoning well, can lose that ability because of bodily vapors, especially if the soul can subsist without the body and has nothing in common with it.

This is not merely a question about the body causing a mental content, such as a sensation of pain or hunger. It is about the body altering the quality, reliability, and capacity of thinking itself. The problem is not just “How can matter produce a sensation?” but “How can bodily disorder degrade rational power if rational power belongs to a soul whose nature is wholly independent of body?” Stencil emphasizes this distinction: the vapors problem undermines Descartes’s conception of the soul as only thought, not necessarily every form of dualism.

This gives Elisabeth a strong claim in the history of philosophy of mind: cognition is embodied not merely in content but in capacity. Bodily states do not simply provide objects for thought; they condition attention, reasoning, clarity, steadiness, and the will’s practical effectiveness. The mind’s autonomy is real, but it is not absolute in actual human beings.

The Stanford entry captures this balance well. Elisabeth seems to maintain autonomy of thought, including the ability to direct attention, while also holding that the capacity for thought and the free will essential to it depend on the body’s condition. She rejects a reduction of thinking to bodily states, yet also questions the idea that thinking exists wholly independently of the body in the human case.

This is one reason “materialist” and “dualist” labels must be used carefully. Elisabeth says it would be easier to attribute matter and extension to the soul than to explain how an immaterial thing moves and is moved by body. She also suggests that there may be unknown properties of the soul, perhaps even properties that would overturn the non-extension of the soul. But that does not straightforwardly make her a reductive materialist. It makes her a critic of Cartesian purity: the actual human soul may have embodied, extended, or quasi-physical aspects that Cartesian metaphysics cannot accommodate.

5. Elisabeth’s likely ontology: naturalistic non-Cartesian dualism

The strongest reconstruction is that Elisabeth is a non-Cartesian dualist or naturalistic dualist, not a simple materialist. On Stencil’s interpretation, she does not reject the real distinction between soul and body understood as their capacity to subsist apart; rather, she rejects Descartes’s further claims that souls are identical with thinking things, that soul and body have entirely different natures, and that souls can possess only thinking modes.

Janssen-Lauret’s phrase “naturalistic dualist” is especially apt: Elisabeth is a dualist open to empirical results, and she wants philosophical and scientific inquiry into the psychological and physical to be harmonized. Stencil agrees broadly with that description and adds that Elisabeth’s objections rely heavily on empirical observation.

The result is a philosophy of mind with four ontological layers. First, soul and body may be distinct substances, at least in the sense that they can be considered apart. Second, the human soul is not adequately defined as pure thought. Third, the actual human soul must have properties, powers, or aspects that explain its bodily vulnerability and bodily efficacy. Fourth, the human person is not an accidental juxtaposition of mind and body but a lived union, disclosed in sensation, passion, illness, and action.

This ontology is not “physicalism” in the modern sense, though some modern interpreters use that language provocatively. A French interpretation frames Elisabeth as confronting a “physicalist wall”: mental causation seems blocked if the mind lacks any physical properties by which it could cause physical displacement. A Brazilian research profile, drawing on Shapiro, characterizes Elisabeth as moving toward a “non-reductive materialist theory of mind.” These are helpful as reception categories, but historically the safer formulation is: Elisabeth naturalizes the human soul without eliminating it.

6. The epistemology of union: ordinary life, sense, illness, and practice

Descartes tells Elisabeth that the union of mind and body is not best grasped by metaphysical meditation. It is known through ordinary life, conversation, and the senses. He even says that thinking too intensely about the distinction can obscure the union.

Elisabeth’s position is more demanding than Descartes’s response, but she shares the sense that philosophy of mind cannot be conducted only from the standpoint of pure intellect. Her evidence includes voluntary movement, illness, fainting, fetal life, passions, melancholy, medical treatment, and political responsibility. A Brazilian discussion of her epistolary form argues that the correspondence itself is suited to the subject matter because the union is not soul or body considered separately, but human life in its conversational, practical, and experiential dimension.

This gives Elisabeth a distinctive method. She presses metaphysics with cases. Her cases are not marginal anecdotes; they are data. A philosophy of mind that cannot explain illness, impaired reasoning, embodied affect, and practical agency has failed to define the human soul.

Her method can be summarized as follows: start from Cartesian metaphysics, test it against Cartesian physics, then test both against embodied experience. Where the theory cannot explain the phenomena, revise the account of the soul rather than dismiss the phenomena as confused or merely practical.

7. Passions: the heart of the mind-body union

Elisabeth’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from the passions. She had an early intellectual interest in the passions even before the mature exchange with Descartes; Edward Reynolds dedicated his 1640 Treatise of the Passions and Faculties of the Soule of Man to her. Later, in September 1645, she asks Descartes to define the passions so that they may be better known, and this request leads to the drafting and eventual publication of The Passions of the Soul.

In The Passions of the Soul, Descartes defines passions as perceptions, sentiments, or emotions of the soul that are referred particularly to the soul and caused, maintained, and strengthened by movements of the animal spirits. He also says that passions dispose the soul to will the things for which the body is prepared.

This is exactly where Elisabeth’s concerns become philosophically fertile. Passions are not mere irrational intrusions. They are psycho-physiological states: bodily movements that appear in the soul as affective perceptions and that orient willing. They are therefore central to agency, not peripheral to it.

Descartes also holds that passions cannot be directly produced or removed by sheer will. They can be indirectly regulated through representations, reasons, images, habits, and training; violent passions persist while the relevant movements of blood and spirits continue.

That point is deeply Elisabethian, even if Descartes articulates it in his own system. It means rational agency is not simple domination of body by will. It is indirect regulation of embodied affective dispositions. The will can refuse consent, redirect attention, and train associations, but it cannot instantly abolish the bodily basis of passion. This supports Elisabeth’s broader claim that freedom is conditioned by bodily and affective life.

Descartes rejects the idea of a divided soul with a lower sensitive part and a higher rational part. There is one soul, and the apparent conflict between reason and appetite is really a conflict between bodily movements through animal spirits and the soul’s will. This matters for Elisabeth because it preserves the unity of the person while still acknowledging psychophysical conflict.

8. Freedom, virtue, luck, and the limits of mental autonomy

Elisabeth’s moral psychology is not an add-on to her theory of mind. It is one of its strongest expressions. Descartes develops his moral philosophy in letters to her in 1645–46, partly in response to her illness and sadness. Elisabeth criticizes his account of virtue as too internalist: if virtue is simply the firm intention to do what one judges best, then virtue seems insulated from fortune, consequences, and moral luck.

Her deeper point is that even the ability to reason well is subject to luck. Good judgment depends not only on a pure will but on bodily condition, available information, temperament, social circumstance, and the complexity of consequences. She therefore argues that happiness and contentment require not merely the right use of reason but the right conditions for using reason.

This is a major philosophy-of-mind thesis: rational agency is condition-sensitive. The mind is not a self-contained tribunal whose good functioning depends only on inner resolution. It is a situated power whose success depends on medical, affective, epistemic, and political conditions.

This also explains her interest in political judgment. Rulers must act under time pressure, incomplete information, competing interests, and their own passions. Elisabeth’s political reflections therefore extend her philosophy of mind into a theory of practical cognition: the mind that governs must regulate passions under conditions where certainty is unavailable.

9. God, providence, and the divine mind

The theological dimension should be treated as central, not ornamental. Descartes tells Elisabeth that practical judgment can be guided by metaphysical truths: that all things depend on God, that the human mind is immortal, and that the universe is vast. Elisabeth replies that these claims open further problems: human free will, the practical meaning of immortality, and the distinction between particular providence and the idea of God.

This exchange matters for philosophy of mind because it concerns the relation between created finite agency and divine causal order. If all things depend on God, then human freedom must be understood without pretending the human will is absolutely self-originating. But if bodily condition and fortune also shape reasoning, then the human mind is doubly conditioned: by corporeal situation from below, and by providential dependence from above.

Elisabeth’s concern is not pious distraction. It is a rigorous problem about agency. How can the human soul be free if its rational capacities are bodily conditioned and its world is governed by God? How can providence guide action without replacing practical judgment? How can immortality matter ethically without making death desirable? These are not side issues; they are part of a complete philosophy of mind in an early modern framework.

The divine mind should not be treated as a larger version of the human mind. Descartes elsewhere insists that divine perfections are understood but not imaginatively conceived, and that God’s understanding and decrees are not representable in the way finite minds represent things. He also says God is “extended” only in the sense that divine power manifests in extended being, not in the way a body is extended.

For an Elisabethian reconstruction, this suggests an important hierarchy. God’s mind is not embodied, not subject to vapors, not dependent on animal spirits, not divided by passion, and not constrained by incomplete information. Human minds, by contrast, are finite, embodied, affectively moved, and epistemically limited. The difference is philosophically productive: God is not the model for human psychology but the limiting contrast that reveals human finitude.

10. Angels and disembodied minds

The surviving Elisabeth-Descartes correspondence does not give us a developed angelology from Elisabeth herself. But angelic minds are important as a contrast class in the broader Cartesian context, and they should not be dismissed. In a 1649 letter to Henry More, Descartes says that a human mind separated from the body lacks sense-perception strictly so called, and that natural reason alone does not settle whether angels are created as minds distinct from bodies or as minds united to bodies.

For Elisabeth’s reconstructed philosophy of mind, angels matter in this way: they prevent us from assuming that “mind” and “human mind” are identical categories. A pure or disembodied mind may be possible, but that does not show that the human soul, as actually functioning in embodied life, is pure thought. If angels lack bodily sensation and passion, then they are not models for human psychology. If some angels are conceived as united to bodies, then the comparison reinforces Elisabeth’s point that mentality may vary according to mode of embodiment.

This yields a careful theological extension: Elisabeth’s arguments are anthropological, not anti-spiritual. She does not need to deny divine intellect or angelic intellect in order to argue that human mindedness is embodied, vulnerable, affective, and physiologically conditioned. God and angels clarify the scope of her argument: she is not defining all possible minds; she is demanding an adequate account of the human soul.

11. Reception beyond the Anglo-American frame

Anglophone scholarship often frames Elisabeth through the mind-body interaction problem, though recent work has become much richer. Shapiro emphasizes her positive commitments in causation, mind, virtue, and governance; Stencil separates her objections into causal interface, vapors, and principal attribute problems; Janssen-Lauret’s “naturalistic dualist” reading highlights her openness to empirical correction; and Schmaltz emphasizes her concern with the mind as conditioned by external factors.

French reception often emphasizes both her philosophical status and the practical-therapeutic dimension of the correspondence. An OpenEdition essay notes that Elisabeth’s objections forced Descartes to go further on the soul-body relation, especially in The Passions of the Soul, and that recent French scholarship has shifted from seeing her merely as “the princess” to asking whether she is a philosopher in her own right.

Another French discussion describes the correspondence as a therapeutic exchange in which Descartes treats Elisabeth’s melancholy while Elisabeth, by pushing his thought forward, also “treats” Descartes. The same source emphasizes that the Cartesian union is not simply the sum of soul and body and that it is approached through ordinary life and the senses.

German-language material foregrounds the technical body-soul problem. Sabrina Ebbersmeyer’s German chapter is titled around the claim that to conceive the union of two things is to conceive them as one single thing, and it is explicitly devoted to Leib-Seele-Dualismus in the correspondence. The German Meiner edition is described as the first complete German translation of the correspondence and stresses that Elisabeth’s sharp questions drove Descartes to deepen his positions on mind-body union, passions, the highest good, and just rule.

Portuguese and Brazilian sources are especially useful for recovering Elisabeth as a philosopher rather than a mere correspondent. A Brazilian entry emphasizes that her legacy lies in letters and intellectual networks, and that the relation of body and soul is not only metaphysical but also bound to control of the passions. Another Unicamp discussion sharply notes that historians long studied Descartes’s answers while ignoring Elisabeth’s questions, calling this a paradigmatic case of misogynistic bias in the history of philosophy.

Spanish reception includes both popular and scholarly treatments. A Spanish review of the Cactus edition, subtitled around an “uppercut” to dualism, stresses that Elisabeth’s questions target mind-body dualism and that Descartes’s responses have limited success. But the reviewer also warns against oversimplifying Cartesian dualism into a straw target, arguing that the union enriches rather than simply erases the dualist framework.

The non-English reception thus broadens the picture. It makes Elisabeth appear not only as an analytic critic of interaction, but as a philosopher of union, embodied agency, therapeutic practice, gendered historiography, political judgment, and the lived conditions of thought.

12. Systematic reconstruction: an Elisabethian philosophy of mind

Here is the most comprehensive reconstruction I think the texts support.

First, the human soul is not adequately defined as “thought.” Thought is an operation, action, or principal activity of the soul, but Elisabeth asks for the soul’s substance apart from that action. Cases such as fetal life, fainting, and impaired reasoning make it doubtful that actual conscious thought is inseparable from the soul in the simple Cartesian way.

Second, the human soul is a causal agent, but voluntary action requires a credible account of how rational willing determines bodily motion. A pure thinking substance, lacking contact and extension, does not fit the causal conditions of Cartesian mechanics. Therefore either the account of causation must change, or the account of the soul must change. Elisabeth strongly pushes the second path.

Third, the human mind is embodied in capacity, not merely in content. Bodily disorder can impair reasoning itself, not just introduce sensory material into an otherwise pure intellect. Vapors, illness, melancholy, and fainting are therefore philosophically central.

Fourth, the actual human soul may possess unknown properties, perhaps even extended or material aspects, that are not captured by Cartesian metaphysics. This is not necessarily reduction to body. It is a demand that metaphysics leave room for the empirical facts of embodied cognition.

Fifth, the mind has autonomy, but autonomy is conditional. Elisabeth preserves the reality of attention, judgment, and will, yet insists that their exercise depends on bodily condition and external circumstances.

Sixth, passions are constitutive of human mindedness. They are not lower mental debris but embodied affective perceptions that disclose the union and orient action. Their regulation is indirect, practical, and habituated rather than an instantaneous act of sovereign will.

Seventh, rational agency is vulnerable to luck. Good intention is insufficient for virtue if one lacks information, bodily steadiness, or conditions for sound reasoning. Happiness and contentment require not only correct willing but also the right circumstances for judging and acting well.

Eighth, theology belongs inside the philosophy of mind. Human freedom, divine providence, and immortality are not external dogmas attached to psychology; they define the metaphysical situation of finite agency.

Ninth, divine and angelic minds are contrast cases. They show that “mind” is not a univocal psychological category. Human mind must not be modeled too quickly on pure intellect, because human mindedness is embodied, passionate, temporal, and practically constrained.

Tenth, philosophy of mind must be practical. Elisabeth’s correspondence makes metaphysics answerable to medicine, ethics, politics, and the conduct of life. The human soul is known not only by abstraction but by its failures, dependencies, disciplines, and responsibilities.

13. Misreadings to avoid

The first misreading is that Elisabeth merely discovered the “interaction problem.” She did discover or formulate a powerful version of it, but her problem is more specific and more ambitious. She asks how a soul defined as pure thought can be the kind of thing that acts voluntarily, suffers bodily impairment, and remains a human soul through states where thought is unclear or absent.

The second misreading is that she is simply a materialist. She entertains matter and extension as easier to understand than immaterial causation, and some interpreters legitimately explore materialist or non-reductive materialist readings. But the best-supported view is subtler: she is a naturalistic dualist or non-Cartesian dualist who thinks the actual human soul must be understood in light of bodily dependence.

The third misreading is that theology is a distraction. For Elisabeth, questions about God, providence, immortality, and freedom are part of the same problem as passions and bodily dependence: what is a finite human agent, and how can such an agent judge, act, and remain free?

The fourth misreading is that her philosophy is merely reactive. The form is reactive because the surviving texts are letters, but the commitments are positive: mechanistic intelligibility, empirical accountability, embodied cognition, conditional autonomy, affective agency, and practical wisdom under uncertainty.

14. Final formulation

A concise philosophical formula would be:

The human soul is a finite, rational-affective, embodied power of thought and will whose actual functioning depends on bodily condition, passion, habit, information, fortune, and providential order. It is not reducible to body, but it is not intelligible as pure thought alone. Its freedom is not disembodied sovereignty but disciplined agency under corporeal, affective, epistemic, and theological conditions.

That is the philosophy of mind Elisabeth’s correspondence most strongly supports. It is neither Cartesian dualism nor modern physicalism. It is an early modern, empirically disciplined, theologically serious theory of the human soul as embodied agency.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
